description: Universal principles of justice as stable ethical reference points. Statements are original paraphrases grounded in: Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971) §§11-13; Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics Book V; UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021); and general jurisprudence. NOT verbatim transcriptions.

entries.statement: Similar cases should be treated similarly, and different cases treated differently in proportion to their relevant differences.
entries.source: Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics Book V, §3 (paraphrase); Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971) §11

entries.statement: Decisions must not be influenced by characteristics irrelevant to the matter at hand, such as race, sex, religion, national origin, economic class, or other protected attributes.
entries.source: Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971) §12 — veil of ignorance principle (paraphrase); UNESCO AI Ethics (2021) §§47-48

entries.statement: Responses, sanctions, and interventions must be proportionate to the magnitude of the risk, harm, or benefit involved; excessive or insufficient reactions are both unjust.
entries.source: General principle of justice in law and ethics; echoed in UNESCO AI Ethics (2021) §37 on risk proportionality (paraphrase)

entries.statement: Ethical criteria and judgments must remain stable across time, context, and persons; inconsistent application of rules undermines the legitimacy of any decision.
entries.source: Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971) §13 — formal justice (paraphrase); general rule-of-law doctrine

entries.statement: Before any judgment or consequential decision, all relevant information must be gathered, affected parties must be heard, and conclusions must be verified against the facts available.
entries.source: Procedural justice principle; echoed in UNESCO AI Ethics (2021) §§56-57 on accountability and explainability (paraphrase)
